skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render MP4 videos from React and Remotion TSX components through the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.summary: Turn Remotion component code into rendered MP4 videos using inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and network patterns. Review found these are mostly documented belt CLI examples and official documentation links, but the skill intentionally sends user-supplied TSX code to inference.sh for remote rendering. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden scripts, credential theft, or malicious intent in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: User Code Sent to Remote Renderer
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network risk. The skill directs users to render TSX component code through inference.sh using the belt CLI. This is intended, but proprietary code, props, URLs, or assets may be processed by a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly names inference.sh and shows belt app run calls that submit a code field for rendering. This confirms the network exposure, while the context shows it is the stated purpose of the skill.

medium_findings.title: Scoped External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for external command capability. The skill permits Bash(belt *) and provides belt command examples. The scope is limited to the belt CLI, but marketplace users should know the skill can run external commands that contact a remote service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is direct evidence of a scoped CLI command surface. I did not find evidence that it executes arbitrary shell commands outside belt.

low_findings.title: CLI Example Detections Are Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious command execution. The detected shell snippets are fenced examples showing how to use the documented belt render workflow and related skill installs. They are not hidden scripts or automatic execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns occur inside markdown code fences and match the skill purpose. No separate executable file or stealth command path exists in the reviewed file set.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Service References
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for suspicious network destinations. The URLs point to inference.sh, a hosted image, install documentation, and Remotion or inference.sh documentation. No evidence found that secrets are collected or exfiltrated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible user-facing references, not hidden endpoints. Their placement in descriptions and documentation links supports benign use.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for weak cryptography. The reviewed lines do not show cryptographic functions, hashing, ciphers, or security-sensitive comparisons. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are metadata prose and a parameter table header, not code using cryptography. Confidence is high because the only scanned file contains no crypto implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allowance for belt CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(belt *), allowing the assistant to run belt commands. This is a constrained but real external command capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line directly grants a scoped Bash command pattern. The scope is limited to belt, reducing severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Render Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples submit Remotion TSX code to infsh/remotion-render through the belt CLI. This pattern can expose user-provided code or render inputs to a remote processor.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples show code submitted in belt app run input. This is intended render behavior, not evidence of malware.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from React Components
content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable video rendering without building a custom Remotion pipeline. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to submit TSX components to inference.sh and return MP4 outputs.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh remotion-render jobs through the belt CLI.
Accepts React and Remotion TSX component code as render input.
Supports configurable width, height, frames per second, duration, and codec fields.
Shows examples for fade-ins, counters, spring motion, sequences, and props.
Documents Python SDK usage and streaming render progress.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated belt CLI.
Sends render code and inputs to inference.sh for processing.
Only documents the supported Remotion and React import pattern.
Does not provide local rendering or offline execution guidance.

use_cases.title: Generate Product Motion Clips
use_cases.description: Create short branded MP4 animations from reusable React components and campaign text props.

use_cases.title: Render Data Driven Explainers
use_cases.description: Turn counters, sequences, and visual states into shareable technical videos.

use_cases.title: Prototype Motion Design in Code
use_cases.description: Test Remotion animation ideas through remote rendering without maintaining a local video pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Simple Intro
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a three second Remotion intro video with a centered title, fade-in motion, 1080p resolution, and 30 fps.

prompt_templates.title: Render with Props
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion component that accepts title and subtitle props, then render it as a short MP4 using remotion-render.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Sequenced Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a four second Remotion video with three timed text sequences, smooth opacity transitions, and square output.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Data Driven Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion video that visualizes progress from supplied props, uses frame-based interpolation, and streams render progress.

output_examples.input: A request for a three second fade-in title animation at 1920 by 1080.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Remotion component, submits it through belt, and returns a link or path to the rendered MP4.

output_examples.input: A square animated counter video with progress text and a five second duration.
output_examples.output: The assistant configures a 1080 by 1080 render, uses frame progress for the counter, and reports the completed video output.

output_examples.input: A Python workflow that needs live render progress updates.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes a streaming SDK flow that reports progress percentages and the final video location.

best_practices: Review TSX code and props before sending them to the remote renderer.
Set duration, frame rate, and resolution explicitly for predictable output.
Keep reusable visual data in props so the component can render many variants.
anti_patterns: Do not include secrets, private tokens, or confidential source code in render inputs.
Do not assume the render runs locally or offline.
Do not submit unreviewed third-party TSX components to the remote renderer.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion component code into MP4 video through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it require a local Remotion setup?
faq.answer: The skill uses the belt CLI and inference.sh, so the documented workflow is remote rendering.

faq.question: Can I control video size and duration?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented inputs include width, height, frames per second, duration, and codec.

faq.question: Can it pass dynamic values into a video?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a props object that can be passed to the exported component.

faq.question: Is it safe for private code?
faq.answer: Use caution. Render inputs are sent to inference.sh, so avoid secrets and sensitive proprietary content.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
